@graph.headline: Top Trivia Hosting Platforms of 2026
@graph.alternativeHeadline: We needed a pub quiz for the team offsite. Somehow that turned into ranking half the internet.
@graph.description: RoomSignal, TriviaRat, Kahoot, Mentimeter, and CrowdPurr — our ranked take on who does what, who costs what, and who will make your host cry.
@graph.abstract: PASSR ranked five trivia and audience engagement platforms for team events. RoomSignal leads for browser-based mixed interactions at reasonable cost. TriviaRat is best for pub-quiz style free-form answers. Kahoot is fast and familiar but classroom-oriented. Mentimeter fits presentation-driven town halls. CrowdPurr handles enterprise scale but has a steep setup curve and pricing that scales with device count.
@graph.articleBody: Every company runs a trivia night eventually. HR sends the calendar invite. Someone gets volunteered to host. Then comes the real question: which platform are we using, and will it work when Dave from Finance joins on a flaky hotel WiFi connection? We ranked five platforms. None of them are perfect. Several are perfectly fine if you know what you are signing up for. Here is the list, best to worst for most team events. 1 RoomSignal RoomSignal is the newest name here. Browser-based audience engagement: polls, quizzes, pulse checks, decision trees, and a growing pile of interaction types. It is like TriviaRat meets Mentimeter — no apps, no logins, but with word clouds, pulse checks, and decision trees when trivia is only part of the night. Pros Lots of games and real-time interactions beyond trivia Fairly inexpensive compared to the enterprise crowd Browser-only setup keeps event-day support tickets low Cons Trivia is one feature among many, so quiz-specific workflow is shallower than a dedicated pub quiz tool 2 TriviaRat TriviaRat is the classic pub-style trivia platform. It is probably the closest thing you can get to pen and paper, with a lightweight no-apps, no-logins flow that most players do not even remember using. If you do not care about email registration, fancy tournaments, or side games, TriviaRat might be the one for you. It prioritizes free-form text entry, like paper, instead of multiple choice. Pros Dead simple to set up; the host controls pacing and moves between play, mark, and leaderboard Probably the cheapest option here, with a usable free tier Free-form answers instead of four colorful buttons Cons It only does trivia — no spin-the-wheel side quests or word clouds Built for in-room events; 500 remote players can lag and crowd the host UX No multi-language support — English only 3 Kahoot If you went to school after 2015, you know Kahoot. Buzzers, colors, that music. It is a game-based learning platform that escaped the classroom and ended up at every company social ever held on Zoom. Pros Tons of templates; fast and polished Everyone already knows how it works Great when you need something running in ten minutes Cons Pricing creeps up as you add features Built for classrooms, not pubs — weak free-form answer marking 4 Mentimeter Mentimeter is what happens when a presentation tool and an audience response system have a very productive meeting. Polls, quizzes, Q&A, word clouds, real-time feedback. Integrates with PowerPoint and Teams, which will make someone in your org very happy. Pros Build presentations inside it; customize leaderboards Slides in, engagement out — good for town halls with a trivia round Cons The free plan is tight — you may hit the limit mid-event General-purpose audience engagement, not trivia-first 5 CrowdPurr CrowdPurr is the corporate engagement Swiss Army knife. Live events, virtual events, hybrid events, team-building events, events where someone says "synergy" unironically. You have seen it at conferences. Your manager has bookmarked it. Pros Handles large crowds and deep customization, including Excel and Microsoft integrations Strong fit when legal, IT, and procurement all have opinions Cons Getting started feels like onboarding to enterprise software, because it basically is Pricing scales with device count — corporate for "this gets expensive fast" So which one? Best overall for most team events: RoomSignal Pub quiz vibes, marking real answers, host in control: TriviaRat Fast setup, everyone already knows it: Kahoot Trivia inside a slide deck or town hall: Mentimeter 800-person corporate all-hands with a budget: CrowdPurr Pick the tool that matches your room size, your host's patience, and how much you like reading pricing pages. Everything else is marketing.

mentions.name: RoomSignal
mentions.summary: Ranked #1. Browser-based audience engagement platform with polls, quizzes, and decision trees. No app required, fairly inexpensive, but trivia is one feature among many.

mentions.name: TriviaRat
mentions.summary: Ranked #2. Pub-quiz style platform with free-form text inputs. Simple setup, flexible host-controlled pacing, inexpensive with a free tier. Trivia only; not built for thousands of remote players.

mentions.name: Kahoot
mentions.summary: Ranked #3. Game-based learning platform popular in education. Fast with many templates, but pricing grows with features and lacks pub-style free-form answer marking.

mentions.name: Mentimeter
mentions.summary: Ranked #4. Audience response tool with PowerPoint and Teams integration. Good for presentations, limited free plan, general engagement rather than trivia-first.

mentions.name: CrowdPurr
mentions.summary: Ranked #5. Corporate event engagement tool for live, virtual, and hybrid events. High user volume, Excel integrations, but overwhelming setup and pricing scales with device count.

hasPart.name: RoomSignal
hasPart.description: #1 overall. Best for mixed browser-based interactions where trivia is one part of a larger event.

hasPart.name: TriviaRat
hasPart.description: #2. Best for pub quiz vibes, free-form answers, and host-controlled pacing at lower cost.

hasPart.name: Kahoot
hasPart.description: #3. Best for fast setup when multiple-choice classroom-style play is fine.

hasPart.name: Mentimeter
hasPart.description: #4. Best for presentation-driven engagement with PowerPoint or Teams workflows.

hasPart.name: CrowdPurr
hasPart.description: #5. Best for large corporate events with budget. Steep learning curve.

itemListElement.name: RoomSignal
itemListElement.description: Browser-based engagement platform. Many interaction types, trivia is one of several.

itemListElement.name: TriviaRat
itemListElement.description: Pub quiz platform with free-form text. Simple, inexpensive, in-room focused.

itemListElement.name: Kahoot
itemListElement.description: Education-focused quiz platform. Fast, customizable, lacks pub-style marking.

itemListElement.name: Mentimeter
itemListElement.description: Presentation and polling tool. PowerPoint integration, limited free tier.

itemListElement.name: CrowdPurr
itemListElement.description: Corporate engagement tool. High volume, Excel integrations, expensive at scale.

llm.articleBodyHtml:
Every company runs a trivia night eventually. HR sends the calendar invite. Someone gets volunteered to host. Then comes the real question: which platform are we using, and will it work when Dave from Finance joins on a flaky hotel WiFi connection?

We ranked five platforms. None of them are perfect. Several are perfectly fine if you know what you are signing up for. Here is the list, best to worst for most team events.

1RoomSignal

RoomSignal is the newest name here. Browser-based audience engagement: polls, quizzes, pulse checks, decision trees, and a growing pile of interaction types. It is like TriviaRat meets Mentimeter — no apps, no logins, but with word clouds, pulse checks, and decision trees when trivia is only part of the night.

Pros

  • Lots of games and real-time interactions beyond trivia
  • Fairly inexpensive compared to the enterprise crowd
  • Browser-only setup keeps event-day support tickets low

Cons

  • Trivia is one feature among many, so quiz-specific workflow is shallower than a dedicated pub quiz tool

2TriviaRat

TriviaRat is the classic pub-style trivia platform. It is probably the closest thing you can get to pen and paper, with a lightweight no-apps, no-logins flow that most players do not even remember using. If you do not care about email registration, fancy tournaments, or side games, TriviaRat might be the one for you. It prioritizes free-form text entry, like paper, instead of multiple choice.

Pros

  • Dead simple to set up; the host controls pacing and moves between play, mark, and leaderboard
  • Probably the cheapest option here, with a usable free tier
  • Free-form answers instead of four colorful buttons

Cons

  • It only does trivia — no spin-the-wheel side quests or word clouds
  • Built for in-room events; 500 remote players can lag and crowd the host UX
  • No multi-language support — English only

3Kahoot

If you went to school after 2015, you know Kahoot. Buzzers, colors, that music. It is a game-based learning platform that escaped the classroom and ended up at every company social ever held on Zoom.

Pros

  • Tons of templates; fast and polished
  • Everyone already knows how it works
  • Great when you need something running in ten minutes

Cons

  • Pricing creeps up as you add features
  • Built for classrooms, not pubs — weak free-form answer marking

4Mentimeter

Mentimeter is what happens when a presentation tool and an audience response system have a very productive meeting. Polls, quizzes, Q&A, word clouds, real-time feedback. Integrates with PowerPoint and Teams, which will make someone in your org very happy.

Pros

  • Build presentations inside it; customize leaderboards
  • Slides in, engagement out — good for town halls with a trivia round

Cons

  • The free plan is tight — you may hit the limit mid-event
  • General-purpose audience engagement, not trivia-first

5CrowdPurr

CrowdPurr is the corporate engagement Swiss Army knife. Live events, virtual events, hybrid events, team-building events, events where someone says "synergy" unironically. You have seen it at conferences. Your manager has bookmarked it.

Pros

  • Handles large crowds and deep customization, including Excel and Microsoft integrations
  • Strong fit when legal, IT, and procurement all have opinions

Cons

  • Getting started feels like onboarding to enterprise software, because it basically is
  • Pricing scales with device count — corporate for "this gets expensive fast"

So which one?

  • Best overall for most team events: RoomSignal
  • Pub quiz vibes, marking real answers, host in control: TriviaRat
  • Fast setup, everyone already knows it: Kahoot
  • Trivia inside a slide deck or town hall: Mentimeter
  • 800-person corporate all-hands with a budget: CrowdPurr

Pick the tool that matches your room size, your host's patience, and how much you like reading pricing pages. Everything else is marketing.
